docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: This documentation provides a comprehensive, end-to-end guide for setting up, deploying, running, and maintaining the Bull8 UAT environment on an offline Linux server. The goal of this guide is to establish a clear, repeatable, and production-aligned deployment process that can be followed consistently across teams and environments without relying on internet connectivity during installation or execution.

docs.title: Intended Audience
docs.text: This guide is written to support developers, DevOps engineers, and system administrators, providing a structured and practical reference that follows industry-standard deployment practices. It focuses not only on installation steps but also on operational clarity — explaining how components interact, how services should be initialized, and how to validate each stage of the deployment.

docs.title: What This Guide Covers
docs.text:
This documentation covers the complete lifecycle of deployment, organized in a step-by-step format so that the entire environment can be recreated reliably from scratch whenever required.

Area Description Environment Preparation System prerequisites and initial server configuration Offline Dependency Handling Installing packages and libraries without internet access Service Initialization Starting and managing required background services Docker-Based Deployment Building, transferring, and running application containers Configuration Management Managing environment variables, config files, and secrets Verification Procedures Validating each stage of the deployment Troubleshooting Common issues and resolution steps

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This guide helps you get started quickly.

docs.title: Steps to Setup
docs.text:
Follow the steps below in the given order to successfully install, configure, and run the Bull system. Each step builds on the previous one, so it is recommended not to skip any stage.

This setup process covers system dependencies, core services, database initialization, and application-level components required for end-to-end functionality.

docs.title: Setup Flow
docs.text:
1. Installation of Python Install and configure the required Python version. 👉 Setting Up Python

  2. Installation of Docker Set up Docker for managing containerized services. 👉 Setting Up Docker

  3. Setting Up Core Services Install and configure required backend services:

  4. Redis
  5. NATS
  6. MySQL

  7. ClickHouse 👉 Setting Up Core Services

  8. Creating the Blitz Database Create and initialize the Blitz database schema. 👉 Creating the Blitz Database

  9. Registering Instruments into Redis Load and register instrument data into Redis for fast access. 👉 Registering Instruments into Redis

  10. Installing Python Dependencies (Offline) Install required Python packages using offline bundles. 👉 Offline Python Dependencies

  11. Setting Up Blitz Gateway Configure and deploy the Blitz Gateway service. 👉 Setting Up Blitz Gateway

  12. Setting Up Authentication Server Configure authentication and authorization services. 👉 Setting Up Auth Server

  13. Setting Up Market Data Feed Service Configure and start the market data feed service for real-time data ingestion. 👉 Setting Up Market Data Feed Service

Once all steps are completed, the system should be fully operational and ready for validation and testing.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This section outlines the minimum system requirements needed to successfully set up and run the Bull system.

Before starting the installation process, ensure that your environment meets the required hardware, software, and third-party service prerequisites. Meeting these requirements helps prevent setup issues, improves stability, and ensures optimal system performance.

The following subsections provide detailed information about each requirement category.

docs.title: Requirement Details
docs.text:
• 🖥️ Hardware Requirements
  • 💻 Software Requirements
  • 🔗 Third-Party Services

docs.title: Hardware Requirements
docs.text:
The following hardware specifications define the minimum and recommended requirements for running the Bull system efficiently.

These requirements are designed to support stable operation, handle medium to high workloads, and ensure optimal performance during processing, deployment, and runtime. Systems with higher specifications may deliver improved responsiveness and scalability, especially in production environments.

docs.title: List of Requirements
docs.text:
1. Operating system : CentOS 8
  2. Processor: Intel i7 to i9 (minimum 16 cores)
  3. Minimum Speed: 3.5 GHz (Recommended: above 4.0 GHz)
  4. RAM : 32GB (16GB for Medium Load)
  5. Storage: 500GB HDD or SSD (recommend SSD)

docs.title: Software Requirements
docs.text:
This section outlines the required software components and dependencies needed to install, configure, and run the Bull system successfully.

Ensuring that the correct software versions are installed helps maintain compatibility, security, and stability across environments. It also reduces setup errors and ensures smooth integration with supporting tools and services during development and deployment.

docs.title: List of Requirements
docs.text:
1. Python 3.x
  2. Docker
  3. Docker Compose (v2 Recommended)
  4. Multicast network enabled
  5. PostgreSQL with TimeaScaleDB (manual Installation)
  6. Putty (accesing the CMD)
  7. WinSCP (for trasferring the files)

docs.title: Third Party Services
docs.text:
Follow the steps below in the given order to successfully install, configure, and run the Bull system. Each step builds on the previous one, so it is recommended not to skip any stage.

This setup process covers system dependencies, core services, database initialization, and application-level components required for end-to-end functionality.

docs.title: Setup Flow
docs.text:
1. Installation of Python Install and configure the required Python version along with essential system tools. 👉 Setting Up Python

  2. Installation of Docker Set up Docker to manage containerized services used by the system.

  3. Setting Up Core Services Install and configure the following services:

  4. Redis
  5. NATS
  6. MySQL

  7. ClickHouse

  8. Creating the Blitz Database Initialize the required database schema and core tables used by the system.

  9. Registering Instruments into Redis Load and register instrument data into Redis for fast access and caching.

  10. Installing Python Dependencies (Offline) Install all required Python packages using offline dependency bundles.

  11. Setting Up Blitz Gateway Configure and deploy the Blitz Gateway service for request routing and system communication.

  12. Setting Up Authentication Server Install and configure the authentication service for user and service-level access control.

  13. Setting Up Market Data Feed Service Configure and start the market data feed service to enable real-time data ingestion and processing.

Once all steps are completed, the system should be fully operational and ready for validation and testing.

docs.title: Setting up Core Services(MySql, Redis, Nats, ClickHouse, PostgreSql)
docs.text:
This section covers the initialization of Core services required by the system before the main application components are deployed. These services provide the core infrastructure layer responsible for data storage, caching, messaging, and analytics, and must be configured and running correctly to ensure overall system stability and performance.

The initialization process is managed using a dedicated Docker Compose configuration file:

docker-compose-init.yml

Using this compose file ensures that all foundational services are started in a controlled and consistent manner, allowing the application stack to connect to reliable dependencies from the beginning.

The services initialized in this phase include:

  • MySQL — Primary relational database for transactional data.
  • Redis — In-memory data store used for caching and fast data access.
  • NATS — Lightweight messaging system for event-driven communication.
  • ClickHouse — High-performance analytical database for large-scale data processing.
  • PostgreSQL (TimescaleDB) — Time-series database component requiring manual setup and configuration.

By completing this initialization phase first, the environment is prepared to support application services, background jobs, and analytics workflows in a predictable and maintainable way.

docs.title: Offline Initialization of Third-Party Services Using Docker
docs.text: This guide explains how to prepare and run required third-party services on a Linux server without internet access. Docker images are downloaded on a Windows machine with internet connectivity, transferred to the Linux server, and then loaded and started offline.

docs.title: PART 1 — Prepare Docker Images on Windows (Internet Machine)
docs.text: On your Windows machine:

docs.title: Pull Required Docker Images
docs.text:
Open terminal (PowerShell / CMD) and run:

docker pull redis:latest
docker pull nats:latest
docker pull mysql:latest
docker pull clickhouse/clickhouse-server:latest

docs.title: Export Images (if not already exported)
docs.text:
Save images as .tar files for offline transfer:

docker save -o redis_latest.tar redis:latest
docker save -o nats_latest.tar nats:latest
docker save -o mysql_latest.tar mysql:latest
docker save -o clickhouse_latest.tar clickhouse/clickhouse-server:latest

docs.title: Copy Files to Linux Server
docs.text:
Using WinSCP, transfer the following files:

redis_latest.tar
nats_latest.tar
mysql_latest.tar
clickhouse_latest.tar
docker-compose-init.yml
mysql_create_table_schema.txt
mysql/initdb/
clickhouse-init/
clickhouse-config/
docker-compose-init.yml

Target directory on Linux:

/opt/app/

docs.title: PART 2 — Load Docker Images on Linux (Offline Server)
docs.text:
Login to Linux using PuTTY and navigate to the directory:

cd /opt/app

docs.title: Load Docker Images
docs.text:
docker load -i redis_latest.tar
docker load -i nats_latest.tar
docker load -i mysql_latest.tar
docker load -i clickhouse_latest.tar

docs.title: Verify Loaded Images
docs.text:
docker images

You should see:

redis
nats
mysql
clickhouse/clickhouse-server

docs.title: Verify Docker Installation
docs.text:
docker --version
docker-compose --version

docs.title: Start Services
docs.text:
docker-compose -f docker-compose-init.yml up -d

If containers already exist:

docker-compose down
docker-compose -f docker-compose-init.yml up -d

docs.title: Check Container Status
docs.text:
docker ps

Expected running containers:

blitz_redis
blitz_nats
blitz_mysql
blitz_clickhouse

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
This process enables a complete offline initialization of infrastructure services using Docker:

  • Redis (Caching)
  • NATS (Messaging)
  • MySQL (Primary Database)
  • ClickHouse (Analytics Database)

By preparing images on an internet-connected system and deploying them offline, you ensure repeatable, secure, and controlled environment setup suitable for restricted or production-grade infrastructure.

docs.title: Instrument Registered in Redis
docs.text:
This module is responsible for:

  • Parsing raw instrument files received from NSE & BSE
  • Normalizing and combining them into a single instruments.csv
  • Enriching instruments with derived fields (Instrument IDs, Index instruments, ELM%)
  • Storing the final instruments data into Redis under a single key

It is designed to be run as a CLI application and can be safely re-run multiple times per day.

docs.title: InstrumentsRegisterInRedis — Folder Structure
docs.text:
`InstrumentsRegisterInRedis/
│
├── application.py                # Main CLI entry point
├── instrument_parser.py          # Core parsing & CSV generation logic
├── auto_run.bat                  # Windows helper script
├── ael_elm_updater.py            # Updates ELM% from AEL
├── common.py                     # Enums & shared constants
├── utils.py                      # Utility helpers (token generation, etc.)
│
├── config/
│   └── column_info.py            # Column definitions & rename mappings
│
├── parsers/                      # Exchange-specific parsers
│   ├── bse_parser.py
│   ├── bsecm_parser.py
│   ├── bsefo_parser.py
│   ├── nse_parser.py
│   ├── nsecm_parser.py
│   ├── nsefo_parser.py
│   └── indexes_parser.py
│
├── InstrumentFiles/              # Raw input files (exchange-provided)
│   ├── contract
│   ├── contract_1706
│   ├── contract_11082025
│   ├── EQD.csv
│   ├── indexes
│   ├── SCRIP
│   ├── security
│   ├── security_1706
│   └── security_11082025
│
├── Instruments/                  # Generated output
│   └── instruments.csv
│
├── __pycache__/
└── .gitignore`

docs.title: Root Files
docs.text: File Description application.py Main CLI entry point instrument_parser.py Core parsing & CSV generation logic auto_run.bat Windows helper script ael_elm_updater.py Updates ELM% from AEL common.py Enums & shared constants utils.py Utility helpers (token generation, etc.)

docs.title: Directories
docs.text: Directory Description config/ Column definitions & rename mappings parsers/ Exchange-specific parser modules InstrumentFiles/ Raw input files provided by exchanges Instruments/ Generated output (e.g., instruments.csv)

docs.title: Parsers
docs.text: File Exchange bse_parser.py BSE bsecm_parser.py BSE Capital Market bsefo_parser.py BSE Futures & Options nse_parser.py NSE nsecm_parser.py NSE Capital Market nsefo_parser.py NSE Futures & Options indexes_parser.py Indexes

docs.title: How to Run the Instrument Registration Module
docs.text: !!! info The InstrumentsRegisterInRedis module is executed as a CLI application using application.py. Since the target Linux server does not have internet access, all required Python dependencies must be downloaded on a Windows machine and transferred offline before execution.

docs.title: Step 1 — Prepare Python Dependencies (Windows Machine)
docs.text:
On a Windows system with internet access, create a directory to store offline packages and download all required dependencies listed in requirements.txt.

Dependencies: - apscheduler - requests - redis - pandas - psutil - cryptography - pymysql - sqlalchemy

Download the packages:

pip download -r requirements.txt -d offline_packages

This command downloads all required wheels and source distributions into the offline_packages directory.

docs.title: Step 2 — Transfer Dependencies to the Linux Server
docs.text:
Using WinSCP or any SCP-based tool:

  1. Connect to the Linux server.
  2. Copy the offline_packages directory to a suitable location, for example:
/opt/apps/offline_packages

docs.title: Step 3 — Install Dependencies on the Linux Server (Offline)
docs.text:
On the Linux server, navigate to the directory containing the offline packages and install them:

pip install --no-index --find-links=/opt/apps/offline_packages -r requirements.txt

!!! warning Ensure the correct Python environment is active before installing dependencies.

docs.title: Step 4 — Run the Application
docs.text:
Once all dependencies are installed, navigate to the project root directory and run the application:

python application.py

!!! success The application will parse instrument files, generate the consolidated instruments.csv, and register the final instrument data into Redis.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The application can be safely re-run multiple times per day.
  • Existing Redis data will be updated based on the latest instrument files.
  • Ensure Redis is running and accessible before execution.

docs.title: TP OMS Configuration
docs.text: This section defines the supported Third-Party OMS (Order Management Systems) along with their authentication behavior and required input parameters.

docs.title: 🟢 Kite (Zerodha)
docs.text: OMS Name: Kite Self Login: ✅ Yes

docs.title: Login Configuration
docs.text:
• Login URL: https://kite.zerodha.com/connect/login
  • Token URL: https://api.kite.trade/session/token

docs.title: Required Inputs
docs.text: Field Name Label Type Required Notes UserId User ID Text ✅ Minimum 3 characters Password Password Password ✅ ApiKey API Key Text ✅ ApiSecret API Secret Password ✅ TotpSecret TOTP Secret Password ✅ PrimaryIpAddress Primary IP Address IP Address ✅ SecondaryIpAddress Secondary IP Address IP Address ✅ RedirectUrl Redirect URL Text ✅ Default: http://localhost:8080/

docs.title: 🟢 MOFL
docs.text: OMS Name: MOFL Self Login: ❌ No

docs.title: Required Inputs
docs.text: Field Name Label Type Required Notes ClientId Client ID Text ✅ Minimum 3 characters Password Password Password ✅ ApiKey API Key Text ✅ PrimaryIpAddress Primary IP Address IP Address ✅ SecondaryIpAddress Secondary IP Address IP Address ✅ DOB Date of Birth Date ✅ Format: DD-MM-YYYY

docs.title: 🟢 XTS
docs.text: OMS Name: XTS Self Login: ✅ Yes

docs.title: Required Inputs
docs.text: Field Name Label Type Required Notes UserId User ID Text ✅ Minimum 3 characters BaseURL Base URL Text ✅ ApiKey API Key Text ✅ ApiSecret API Secret Text ✅ TotpSecret TOTP Secret Text ❌ Optional Source Source Text ✅ InteractivePath Interactive Path Text ✅

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Self Login = Yes means the OMS supports direct authentication.
  • Self Login = No means credentials are validated via an external or broker-managed flow.
  • All fields marked Required must be provided during OMS configuration.

docs.title: MySQL Initial Setup
docs.text:
!!! info This section covers the initial MySQL configuration required to prepare the Blitz database after the MySQL container is up and running.

These steps ensure that the database service is accessible and that all required tables are created correctly before proceeding with application setup.

docs.title: 🔐 Step 1 — Access the MySQL Container
docs.text:
Connect to the running MySQL container using Docker:

```bash docker exec -it blitz_mysql mysql -u root -p

docs.title: Setting up Blitz-Gateway
docs.text:
Since the target Linux machine does not have internet access, the Docker image is first built on a Windows machine with internet connectivity, exported as a portable tar file, and then transferred to the Linux server for deployment.

The setup covers building the Docker image, transferring it securely, loading it into the Linux Docker environment, configuring Docker Compose, and running the service as a containerized application. This approach ensures consistent deployments across environments while maintaining isolation, portability, and ease of maintenance.

By following this process, Blitz Gateway can be reliably deployed, restarted, and managed on restricted or secure infrastructure without requiring direct internet access.

docs.title: Part 1: Build Docker Image on Windows
Go to Blitz Gateway Source Code
docs.text:
On Windows (PowerShell / CMD):

cd <path-to-blitz-gateway-project>

You must have:

  • Dockerfile
  • Application source code
  • Config files (if any)

docs.title: Build Docker Image
docs.text:
docker build -t blitz-gateway:latest .

Verify image:

docker images | grep blitz-gateway

docs.title: Save Image as TAR File
docs.text:
docker save blitz-gateway:latest -o blitz-gateway_latest.tar

This file is portable and can be used offline.

docs.title: Part 2: Transfer Image to Linux Machine
Copy Image to Linux
docs.text:
Using WinSCP, copy:

blitz-gateway_latest.tar

To Linux path:

/opt/apps/

docs.title: Login to Linux Server
docs.text:
cd /opt/apps
ls

You should see:

blitz-gateway_latest.tar

docs.title: Load Docker Image
docs.text:
docker load -i blitz-gateway_latest.tar

Verify:

docker images | grep blitz-gateway

Expected output:

blitz-gateway   latest

docs.title: Place Docker Compose File
docs.text:
Ensure the following file exists:

/opt/apps/docker-compose.yml

Example (simplified):

version: "3.8"

services:
  blitz-gateway:
    image: blitz-gateway:latest
    container_name: blitz_gateway
    restart: always
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    env_file:
      - .env
    networks:
      - blitz-network

networks:
  blitz-network:
    driver: bridge

⚠️ Image name must exactly match the loaded image (blitz-gateway:latest)

docs.title: Note: Before Running Blitz Gateawy make blow changes at Config Files
docs.text:

        "minLength": 3

docs.title: Start the Service
docs.text: docker-compose up -d

docs.title: Verify Container
docs.text:
docker ps | grep blitz_gateway

Expected output:

Up ... blitz_gateway

docs.title: Check Logs
docs.text: docker logs -f blitz_gateway

docs.title: Setting Up Python
docs.text:
This guide explains how to install Python 3.10.14 on a Linux server that does not have internet access. The installation is performed by downloading the required files on a Windows machine with internet connectivity and then transferring them to the Linux server.

Phase 1 — Download on Windows (Internet Machine)

  1. Open your browser.

  2. Go to the official Python downloads page:

    https://www.python.org/downloads/source/

  3. Under Stable Releases, download:

Python 3.10.14 → Gzipped source tarball
  1. The downloaded file will be:
Python-3.10.14.tgz

docs.title: Phase 2 — Transfer Files to the Linux Server
docs.text:
Using WinSCP (or similar file transfer tool):

  1. Connect to the Linux server.
  2. Copy the downloaded file to:
/opt/apps

docs.title: Phase 3 — Build and Install Python on Linux
docs.text: Login to the Linux server using PuTTY (SSH).

docs.title: Step 1 — Navigate to the Software Directory
docs.text:
cd /home/dev/softwares
ls

Expected output:

Python-3.10.14.tgz

docs.title: Step 2 — Extract the Source Code
docs.text:
tar -xvf Python-3.10.14.tgz
cd Python-3.10.14

docs.title: Step 3 — Verify Build Tools
docs.text:
Ensure the required build tools are installed:

gcc --version
make --version

Both commands should return version information.

docs.title: Step 4 — Configure the Build
docs.text:
Run the configuration script:

./configure --enable-optimizations

This prepares the environment and checks system dependencies before compilation.

docs.title: Step 5 — Compile the Source
docs.text:
make -j4

This compiles Python using multiple CPU cores for faster build time.

docs.title: Step 6 — Install Safely (Recommended)
docs.text:
sudo make altinstall

altinstall ensures that the system’s default Python version is not overwritten, which is critical for Linux system stability.

docs.title: Installation Locations
docs.text:
After successful installation:

/usr/local/bin/python3.10
/usr/local/bin/pip3.10

System Python remains unchanged:

/usr/bin/python3

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
/usr/local/bin/python3.10 --version

Expected output:

Python 3.10.14

docs.title: (Optional) Add Python to PATH
docs.text:
To make Python easily accessible:

echo 'export PATH=/usr/local/bin:$PATH' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc

Now you can verify using:

python3.10 --version

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This offline installation approach allows Python to be installed safely on internet-restricted Linux servers while preserving system stability. By building from source and using altinstall, you ensure compatibility and avoid conflicts with the system’s default Python environment.
